intro: Rewriting TypeScript code into C drops software from a high-level managed environment directly down to a procedural, close-to-hardware language. Developers execute this optimization step to write resource-constrained firmware, microkernels, or performance-critical application engines where runtime garbage collection cycles cannot be tolerated. The conversion replaces high-level abstractions with precise manual memory layouts and pointer mechanics.

diffs.from: Statically verified compile-time structural patterns featuring complex utility typings and type guards.
diffs.to: Static, weakly enforced nominal type mappings centered around machine words, structs, and implicit pointer coercions.

diffs.from: Modern C-based language layout featuring block scoping, module export keys, and arrow definitions.
diffs.to: Minimalist procedural syntax emphasizing function steps, pointer dereferences, and macro configurations.

diffs.from: Automated memory scanning through high-level engine garbage collection abstractions.
diffs.to: Manual allocation and tracking managed entirely by the engineer utilizing explicit `malloc` and `free` operations.

diffs.from: Native asynchronous architecture backed by a built-in event queue mechanism.
diffs.to: Purely synchronous procedural logic relying on operating system low-level primitives (pthreads) for parallelism.

diffs.from: Context-rich environments for web applications, JSON processing parsing, and dynamic array expansions.
diffs.to: Minimal headers (libc) delivering primitive string manipulators, basic file system access, and raw memory tools.

examples.title: Struct Mapping and Initialization
examples.description: Defining a structured block of memory representing an item.

examples.title: Array Iteration via Pointer Steps
examples.description: Iterating through continuous numeric fields using fixed sizing boundaries.

tips.title: Track Allocated Allocation Blocks
tips.body: C will not automatically clean up variables allocated via heap tools. Every single instance of `malloc`, `calloc`, or `realloc` must match an explicit, strategically positioned `free` block to prevent terminal memory leaks.

tips.title: Guard Array Bounds Manually
tips.body: TypeScript arrays expand dynamically and throw safe errors or return undefined on out-of-bound requests. C arrays are fixed-size segments of raw memory; writing past boundaries corrupts adjacent data blocks or crashes with a segmentation fault.

tips.title: Re-engineer String Architectures
tips.body: C does not have a native string object. Strings are raw arrays of characters ending with a null terminator character (`\0`); manipulations require careful buffer sizing using `strncpy` or `snprintf` to avoid buffer overflows.

tips.title: Avoid Returning Local Pointers
tips.body: Never return a pointer referencing an internal function stack variable from a C function. Once the execution scope closes, the stack frame is reclaimed, leaving you with a dangerous dangling pointer that leads to undefined behavior.
